{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient is a 16-year-old boy with a seizure disorder and cognitive delay, progressive right lower extremity weakness, lack of eye contact, and a close proximity to his mother, suggesting possible developmental or neurobehavioral features. Physical examination reveals a grade 3/6 holosystolic murmur at the cardiac apex, which may indicate a structural cardiac abnormality. Fundoscopic examination shows multinodular, calcified lesions in the retina bilaterally, which is a key finding. This is highly suggestive of a neurocutaneous syndrome. Among the options, the presence of retinal calcifications is a hallmark of tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC). TSC is associated with mutations in either the TSC1 (chromosome 9) or TSC2 (chromosome 16) genes. The clinical features of TSC include seizures, cognitive delay, skin findings (such as angiofibromas, ash-leaf spots), and retinal calcifications. The presence of retinal calcifications, along with seizures and cognitive delay, strongly points to TSC. The holosystolic murmur may be due to cardiac involvement (e.g., rhabdomyoma), which is also seen in TSC. Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) is associated with café-au-lait macules, neurofibromas, and Lisch nodules, but not typically with retinal calcifications. NF2 is associated with bilateral acoustic neuromas and schwannomas, and may have retinal lesions, but not typically multinodular calcifications. VHL syndrome is associated with renal cell carcinoma and hemangioblastomas, and retinal lesions are not a primary feature. Therefore, the most likely diagnosis is tuberous sclerosis complex, due to a mutation in the TSC1 gene on chromosome 9.", "answer_choice": "C"}
